title: Mesopotamian flood traditions — witness comparison
description: Same motif across Sumerian and Akkadian sources — compare in English without treating them as one story.

rows.label: Atrahasis (Akkadian)
rows.english: Human noise disturbs Enlil; gods agree to wipe out mankind after plague and famine fail.

rows.label: Gilgamesh Tablet XI
rows.english: Anu and Enlil complain that human clamor prevents sleep; council sends the flood.

rows.label: ETCSL Flood story (Sumerian)
rows.english: Sumerian flood narrative in a shorter literary setting — overlaps Atrahasis/Gilgamesh motifs.

rows.label: Sumerian King List
rows.english: Antediluvian kings reign impossibly long; then 'the flood swept over' — mythic time marker, not geology.

rows.label: Atrahasis
rows.english: Ea warns indirectly through a reed wall — Enlil must not know the plan openly.

rows.label: Gilgamesh XI
rows.english: Ea tells Utnapishtim (Atrahasis figure) to tear down his house and build a boat.

rows.label: Sumerian Flood story
rows.english: Divine warning to a human survivor — parallel structure, fragmentary in witnesses.

rows.label: Atrahasis
rows.english: Boat with equal length and breadth; animals and family aboard; seven days of storm.

rows.label: Gilgamesh XI
rows.english: Detailed boat measures; six days and seven nights of wind and flood; landing on a mountain.

rows.label: Sumerian Flood story
rows.english: Boat motif present in Sumerian recension — compare line-by-line to Akkadian versions.

rows.label: Atrahasis
rows.english: Gods gather like flies over the sacrifice; Enlil blesses survivors with distant immortality.

rows.label: Gilgamesh XI
rows.english: Utnapishtim and wife kneel; Enlil grants them eternal life at the mouth of the rivers.

rows.label: King List
rows.english: Kingship descends again after the flood — new political age, not repopulation science.

rows.label: Viral claim
rows.english: The flood proves a global cover-up, planetary collision, or alien weather control.

rows.label: Witness read
rows.english: Multiple Mesopotamian literary traditions share a flood motif — theological and narrative, not a single geology report. Compare witnesses; do not merge into one 'hidden history'.
